questions.question: What is the default value of the CSS position property?
questions.explanation: Every element has position: static by default. It follows the normal document flow.

questions.question: Which position value removes an element from the normal document flow?
questions.explanation: Absolute positioning removes the element from the normal document flow. It is positioned relative to its nearest positioned ancestor.

questions.question: What does position: relative do to an element's original space in the layout?
questions.explanation: Relative positioning preserves the element's original space in the layout, even when the element is offset visually.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a valid value for the position property?
questions.explanation: 'floating' is not a valid CSS position value. The valid values are static, relative, absolute, fixed, and sticky.

questions.question: What does position: fixed position an element relative to?
questions.explanation: Fixed positioning positions an element relative to the browser viewport. It stays in place during scrolling.

questions.question: When does position: sticky activate?
options: When the element reaches a scroll threshold
When the parent has overflow: hidden
questions.explanation: Sticky positioning toggles between relative and fixed. It acts as relative until the scroll reaches the defined threshold (e.g., top: 0).

questions.question: What offset property is required for position: sticky to work?
questions.explanation: Sticky positioning requires at least one offset property (top, right, bottom, or left) to define the scroll threshold where it becomes fixed.

questions.question: If no ancestor has a position value set, what does an absolutely positioned element reference?
questions.explanation: If no ancestor has a position value other than static, an absolutely positioned element references the viewport as its containing block.

questions.question: Which of these is a common use case for position: fixed?
options: Navigation bars that stay at top
questions.explanation: Fixed positioning is commonly used for persistent navigation bars that remain visible during scrolling.

questions.question: What happens to an element with position: absolute when its parent has overflow: hidden?
options: It is clipped by the parent
questions.explanation: An absolutely positioned child is clipped if the parent has overflow: hidden, as it still respects the parent's overflow property.

questions.question: Which property controls the stacking order of positioned elements?
questions.explanation: z-index controls the stacking order of positioned elements. Higher values appear on top.

questions.question: What happens when you set position: relative without any offset properties?
questions.explanation: A relatively positioned element without offset properties appears exactly where it would in normal flow, just like static.

questions.question: Which position value creates a new containing block for absolutely positioned descendants?
questions.explanation: Any non-static position value (relative, absolute, fixed, or sticky) establishes a new containing block for absolutely positioned children.

questions.question: How does position: sticky affect the element's original space?
options: Removes it from flow when activated
questions.explanation: Sticky elements always preserve their original space in the layout; only their visual position changes when scrolled.

questions.question: What is a common issue with fixed headers?
options: They overlap page content at the top
questions.explanation: Fixed headers overlap the top of the page content. Usually solved by adding padding-top to the body equal to the header height.

questions.question: Which is more performant for animations: changing top/left or using transform?
questions.explanation: Transform uses GPU compositing and doesn't trigger layout recalculations, making it more performant for animations than changing top/left.

questions.question: What happens when two position: fixed elements overlap without z-index?
options: Later in DOM appears on top
Earlier in DOM appears on top
questions.explanation: Without z-index, positioned elements later in the DOM tree stack on top of earlier ones.

questions.question: Can position: sticky be used inside a container with overflow: hidden?
options: Only if position is also set on parent
questions.explanation: Sticky positioning doesn't work inside containers with overflow: hidden, auto, or scroll because the container clips the element's scroll visibility.

questions.question: What does the inset property do?
options: Sets all offset properties at once
questions.explanation: The inset property is a shorthand for top, right, bottom, and left. E.g., inset: 10px is equivalent to top:10px; right:10px; bottom:10px; left:10px;

questions.question: Which of these is NOT a valid containing block for an absolutely positioned element?
questions.explanation: Floated elements do not establish a containing block for absolutely positioned children. Only positioned (non-static) elements do.
